skill.name: design-an-interface
skill.description: Guide an AI assistant to design several different module or API interfaces, compare trade-offs, and synthesize a better interface shape.
skill.summary: Generate and compare multiple interface designs before implementation.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but review found only Markdown prose and a fenced prompt template. No executable code, shell invocation, cryptographic API use, network access, filesystem access, or prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Prompt Template Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command finding points to a fenced Markdown block that describes a prompt template for sub-agents. It does not execute commands or instruct the model to run shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are inside a Markdown code fence containing plain prompt text. No shell command, Ruby backtick expression, or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prose Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings appear to match words in prose, headings, and checklist text. SKILL.md contains no cryptographic algorithm names, crypto libraries, hashing code, or security-sensitive implementation guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only instructional writing about interface design at these locations. There is no code path where a weak cryptographic primitive could be used.

content.value_statement: Poor interface choices are hard to reverse after implementation starts. This skill helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code generate alternatives, compare trade-offs, and choose a clearer module boundary.
actual_capabilities: Collects requirements for a module, API, or component boundary.
Directs parallel sub-agents to create several distinct interface designs.
Specifies different design constraints for each generated option.
Compares interface simplicity, depth, flexibility, efficiency, and misuse risk.
Guides synthesis of the strongest ideas into a final interface direction.
limitations: It does not implement the selected interface.
It depends on clear requirements from the user.
It is focused on interface shape, not detailed system architecture.
It may require human judgment to select the best trade-off.

use_cases.title: Design a New Internal API
use_cases.description: Explore several caller-facing shapes before building a new service, utility, or library module.

use_cases.title: Review a Proposed Module Boundary
use_cases.description: Compare an existing design against alternatives that hide more complexity or reduce misuse.

use_cases.title: Teach Interface Trade-offs
use_cases.description: Use contrasting designs to explain simplicity, depth, flexibility, and implementation constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Interface Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an interface for a module that handles [module purpose]. The callers are [caller types]. Ask me for missing requirements first.

prompt_templates.title: Compare API Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to generate at least three different interface designs for [module name]. Optimize one for simplicity, one for flexibility, and one for the common case.

prompt_templates.title: Refine an Existing Proposal
prompt_templates.scenario: User already has an interface idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to challenge this interface proposal: [describe proposal]. Generate alternatives, compare trade-offs, and identify what each option hides internally.

prompt_templates.title: Synthesize a Final Direction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to synthesize the best final interface from these options: [summarize options]. Focus on depth, ease of correct use, and future compatibility.

output_examples.input: Design an interface for a feature flag client used by product code and tests.
output: Three interface options with different levels of method count and flexibility.
Usage examples described in plain language for production and test callers.
A prose comparison of simplicity, misuse risk, hidden complexity, and future changes.

output_examples.input: Review my proposed cache abstraction before I implement it.
output: Alternative cache interface shapes that emphasize common operations, extensibility, or strict constraints.
Trade-off notes explaining which design keeps the deepest implementation hidden.
A recommended direction that combines the strongest parts of multiple options.

best_practices: Provide the caller types, common operations, and compatibility constraints before generating designs.
Ask for designs that are meaningfully different, not small variations of one interface.
Choose the final shape based on caller clarity and hidden complexity, not implementation effort alone.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to generate implementation code before choosing an interface.
Do not accept similar sub-agent proposals as sufficient design exploration.
Do not skip the comparison step because the contrast is the main value.

faq.question: What kind of interfaces can this skill design?
faq.answer: It is best for module APIs, library boundaries, service abstractions, and component contracts.

faq.question: Does it write production code?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on interface shape, usage examples, and design trade-offs.

faq.question: Why does it use multiple sub-agents?
faq.answer: Parallel sub-agents help generate genuinely different approaches before comparing them.

faq.question: Can I use it with an existing interface?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the current interface and ask for alternatives that improve clarity or depth.

faq.question: What information should I provide first?
faq.answer: Provide the module purpose, caller types, key operations, constraints, and what should stay hidden.

faq.question: Is this only for backend APIs?
faq.answer: No. It can also help with frontend component APIs and internal tool boundaries.
